front.title: Recommendations for AI-Driven Network Operations Work in the IETF

abstract.t: A growing number of individual Internet-Drafts address the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) in network operations and management. These efforts are currently spread across multiple working groups and mailing lists without a coordinated path. This document surveys the landscape, identifies where work belongs across existing and new venues (OPSAWG, NMOP, NETCONF WG, ONSEN WG, and BoFs approved for IETF 126), and provides guidance to OPS area chairs on how to handle adoption requests for AI-related drafts.

section.title: Introduction
section.t: AI and ML techniques are increasingly being applied to network operations: anomaly detection, closed-loop remediation, intent translation, and autonomous device management. The IETF community has responded with a growing number of individual drafts exploring governance frameworks , network management agent architectures , use cases , and research challenges .
Work on AI-driven network operations should be discussed on the AINETOPS mailing list (ainetops@ietf.org) for general discussion, and brought to OPSAWG for adoption and chartering decisions.

AI and network operations tooling is evolving rapidly. IETF standardization is most valuable for work items that are grounded in stable operational requirements -- audit trails, governance parameters, security practice -- rather than areas where industry tooling is iterating faster than IETF can publish. The proposed work items are chosen with that constraint in mind.

AI agents performing network management do what human operators do, potentially faster and with more data ingestion. This argues for reusing existing IETF protocols and data models rather than creating AI-specific ones, and grounds the scope of the proposed work.

This document identifies where the IETF can make a unique and concrete contribution -- specifically through YANG data models, protocol extensions, and operational guidance -- and provides a dispatch map for OPS area chairs fielding adoption requests for AI-related drafts. It also identifies areas that are better left to research, industry forums, or other bodies.

section.title: Current Landscape
section.t: The following efforts are relevant to AI-driven network operations in the IETF and IRTF:

ul.li: proposes a governance framework of thirteen principles for AI-mediated autonomous network device management, covering human authority, safety, auditability, reversibility, and bounded autonomy.

defines a Network Management Agent (NMA) concept, an AI- driven entity that bridges service intent and network operations using an Intent-Awareness-Analysis-Decision- Execution (IAADE) control loop. The draft identifies three interfaces -- Agent-to-User (A2U), Agent-to-Controller (A2C), and Agent-to-Network (A2N) -- but explicitly defers the definition of the A2C interface to future documents.
documents use cases including reactive troubleshooting, proactive assurance, closed-loop optimization, misconfiguration detection, and virtual operator assistance.
identifies research challenges for coupling AI with network management, including distribution of AI tasks and privacy concerns.
The AINETOPS non-WG mailing list (ainetops@ietf.org) was established to discuss integration of AI and ML in network operations using IETF technologies.

argues that NETCONF has five architectural gaps for AI-driven operations -- AI semantic translation, long-lived cross-domain orchestration, multi-agent consensus, rapid DevOps iteration, and large artifact delivery -- and proposes the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol as complementary layers alongside NETCONF, not replacements for it.

performs a gap analysis of NETCONF and RESTCONF for LLM-driven intent- based networking, identifying the absence of a YANG-to-JSON- Schema automation pipeline as a blocking gap: LLMs cannot reason over YANG tree notation but can consume JSON Schema tool descriptors.

specifies concrete MCP extensions for network equipment management, defining tools that map directly onto YANG datastore operations (get, edit, commit, rollback) and capability advertisement that lets MCP servers declare their supported YANG modules.

proposes a framework for LLM-assisted network management with a mandatory human- in-the-loop audit step before any AI-generated configuration is deployed, and defines YANG modules for both the LLM response record and the human approval workflow.

ONSEN (Operationalizing Network and Service Abstractions) is a new working group being seated for the first time at IETF 126. ONSEN addresses operational abstractions for networks and services, with potential overlap with AI agent governance and operational state work.

section.t: Several BoFs have been approved for IETF 126 that are directly relevant to AI-driven network operations. This document was written before these BoFs were proposed; the following are noted as coordination points rather than definitive routing targets, since not all approved BoFs are WG-forming and not all WG-forming BoFs will form working groups:

ul.li: AUDIT (Agent Use of Delegation and Interaction Traceability): covers delegation mechanisms and interaction traceability for agent systems, overlapping with audit log and attribution work.
CATALIST (Coordinating Agent To Agent List of efforts): covers A2A coordination.
Agent Communication Protocols: covers inter-agent communication protocols, overlapping with A2A and A2C interface work.

Discovery of Agents, Workloads, and Named Entities: covers discovery mechanisms for agent-based systems. There is a genuine technical disagreement in the community about whether existing MCP tool descriptions and JSON Schema are sufficient for capability advertisement, or whether a more formal schema is needed. This BoF is the appropriate venue to resolve that question.
DMSC (Dynamic Multi-agent Secured Collaboration): covers security for multi-agent collaboration, overlapping with the security BCP work.

section.t: Collectively, these efforts have established the problem space and use cases. The next step is to translate this into standards-track work with concrete, interoperable deliverables.

section.title: Where IETF Can Contribute
section.t: The IETF's established strengths lie in defining YANG data models, protocol behavior, and operational guidelines. Applied to AI-driven network operations, this means:

ul.li: Standardizing the data models that describe how AI agents are configured, governed, and monitored -- so that operators can manage AI agents through the same NETCONF/RESTCONF interfaces they already use for network devices.
Extending existing management protocols to carry attribution metadata for AI-initiated changes, enabling interoperable audit trails and rollback.
Producing operational security guidance as a BCP, so that operators deploying AI agents can follow a common baseline regardless of vendor implementation.

Defining the information model for AI agent capabilities, audit trails, and operational state in YANG -- as transport-agnostic schemas that implementations can consume via NETCONF/RESTCONF, gNMI, or other transports. Where protocol extensions to NETCONF or RESTCONF are needed, that work belongs in the NETCONF WG, which is the home for those IETF-owned protocols.

section.t: The IETF is not well-positioned to specify AI algorithms, model architectures, or training methodologies. Those topics belong in the IRTF or in industry forums.

section.title: Working Group Scope
section.t: The volume of AI-related work and the burden of triaging it in existing WGs argues for a dedicated AIOPS working group. OPSAWG should serve as the interim home while the charter is developed with the chairs, the community, and the IESG. The charter should commit to the work items described in this section and explicitly exclude architecture surveys, AI algorithm specification, and gap analyses that stand alone without an associated deliverable.

Experimental and research work related to AI-driven network operations should be directed to the IRTF NMRG. Chartered work items in OPSAWG or a dedicated AIOPS WG are limited to standards-track and BCP deliverables.
The following table maps existing individual drafts to their proposed home for adoption purposes. This is intended as a reference for chairs fielding adoption requests at IETF 126.

tr.td: Coordinate; A2C interface item to NMOP
Informational reference; MCP BCP builds on it
IRTF track; coordinate on audit log

section.t: A use cases and requirements document, building on , should be the first adopted work item. This document will not be published as an RFC but will inform the scope and priorities of the YANG data model work.

section.title: YANG Data Models
section.t: The YANG language is used here as a formal information modeling tool, not as a mandate for NETCONF/RESTCONF as the implementation transport. These schemas are transport-agnostic; implementations may expose them via NETCONF/RESTCONF, gNMI/gRPC, or other mechanisms appropriate to the deployment context. Where protocol extensions to NETCONF or RESTCONF are required, that work belongs in the NETCONF WG. Non-IETF transports such as gRPC, MCP, and REST are valid implementation choices and are out of scope for this charter.

section.title: AI Agent Capability Discovery
section.t: There is an active debate in the community about whether existing mechanisms -- MCP tool descriptions with JSON Schema, skill definitions in natural language -- are sufficient for AI agent capability advertisement in multi-vendor, multi-domain network deployments. Those with hands-on experience building MCP servers and agents report that well-defined tool descriptions work effectively in practice, including for complex tasks. Others argue that natural-language skill names do not scale across vendors without a more formal schema, since capability names are not unique and semantics are not verifiable.

This debate is the right one for the IETF to inform, but it is not resolved in this document. The Discovery of Agents, Workloads, and Named Entities BoF (approved for IETF 126) is the appropriate venue to work through whether and what IETF standardization is needed for agent capability advertisement. Work on this topic should be directed there.

section.title: AI Agent Audit Log YANG Model
section.t: specifies in detail what an audit trail for an AI agent must contain: action timestamps, the anomaly that triggered the action, AI prompts and responses, pre- and post-action state snapshots, the outcome of the action, and whether the action was taken autonomously or required human approval.

This is a well-defined schema. Standardizing it as a YANG data model ensures that AI agent audit logs are interoperable across vendors and consumable by existing operational tooling. Retention periods are a local policy decision; the model should provide a configurable retention parameter without mandating a default value.

Current deployments frequently use OpenTelemetry with Prometheus or OpenMetrics for agent observability. A YANG audit log model provides a normative, vendor-neutral schema for management systems that require a deterministic interface to the audit trail, complementing rather than replacing existing observability infrastructure.

The model should also capture the human-in-the-loop workflow: whether a proposed action required human approval before execution, the identity of the approver, the timestamp of approval or rejection, and any operator annotation attached to the decision. This extends the audit record beyond what the AI agent did autonomously to include the governance interactions that constrained it, enabling post-hoc analysis of where human oversight was exercised and where it was not.

section.title: AI Agent Configuration and Governance YANG Model
section.t: This model targets the management plane controller or orchestrator that oversees AI agents, not the AI agent runtime itself. Not all agent implementations embed a NETCONF/RESTCONF server; this model is intended to be consumed by the operator's existing NMS through the management interface of the orchestration layer.

The governance parameters described in -- the Action Registry, operator-managed Allow List and Block List, rate limit parameters (actions per hour, per-target limits, cooldown periods), protected resource targets, and degradation level thresholds -- are all operator-configured state.

Standardizing these as a YANG data model allows operators to configure and audit AI agent governance through the same NETCONF/RESTCONF interfaces they already use, and enables consistent policy enforcement across heterogeneous deployments.

section.title: AI Agent Operational State YANG Model
section.t: The runtime state of an AI agent -- its current degradation level, active rate-limit counters, retry counts per target, escalation state, and current execution mode (autonomous versus monitoring-only) -- constitutes operational state that operators need to observe.

This model is further informed by the IAADE control loop defined in . The operational state should include the current phase of the IAADE loop (Intent, Awareness, Analysis, Decision, or Execution), the active intent being processed, and for each phase: the inputs consumed, the outputs produced, and any exceptions encountered. This gives operators visibility into not only the health of the AI agent but also what it is currently doing and why.

Standardizing this as a YANG operational state model allows operators to monitor AI agent health and activity through existing telemetry and streaming telemetry infrastructure, using the same tools they use to observe network device state.

section.title: NETCONF/RESTCONF Extensions for AI Agents
section.t: When an AI agent modifies device configuration via NETCONF or RESTCONF, its identity is already captured through the standard session authentication mechanisms that apply to any management plane user. If additional attribution metadata -- such as a flag identifying the session as AI-initiated -- is determined by the community to be warranted for NMS and SIEM correlation, that work belongs in the NETCONF WG, which is the appropriate home for extensions to IETF-owned management protocols. This document does not propose specific extension text.
Any work on NETCONF or RESTCONF extensions to support AI agent use cases belongs in the NETCONF WG.

section.title: MCP Extensions for Network Management
section.t: The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an industry specification maintained by Anthropic, not an IETF standard. IETF does not control its evolution. The IETF's role is limited to producing operational guidance on how to use MCP effectively alongside IETF-defined YANG models and NETCONF/RESTCONF infrastructure.

An informational or BCP document on MCP operational best practices for network management would cover: tool granularity guidance (favor purpose-built operational tools over raw protocol mappings), error code conventions, rollback and confirmation signaling, and capability discovery via ietf-yang-library exposed as an MCP tool. The existing drafts and provide a starting point for this work.
This work is suitable for OPSAWG, in coordination with any AI agent interoperability frameworks that emerge from industry forums.

section.title: Agent-to-Agent and Agent-to-Controller Interfaces
section.t: identifies two interfaces that require standardization in the independent NMA deployment mode: the Agent-to-Controller (A2C) interface between the NMA and an existing SDN controller, and -- implicitly -- peer Agent-to-Agent (A2A) interfaces for multi-agent coordination in heterogeneous deployments.

The CATALIST BoF (Coordinating Agent To Agent List of efforts) and the Agent Communication Protocols BoF, both approved for IETF 126, address A2A and inter-agent communication. If either results in a working group, that is the natural home for this work. If not, NMOP is the fallback, subject to whether the NMOP charter accommodates it -- a question to be resolved with the NMOP chairs, the WG, and the IESG before work is formally proposed there.

The A2C interface should define how the NMA invokes controller capabilities (topology queries, telemetry subscriptions, configuration enforcement) as abstract tools, and how the controller returns results and exceptions to the NMA. The interface should be expressed using existing IETF management protocols (NETCONF or RESTCONF) and YANG data models, avoiding the introduction of a new protocol.

Beyond the single NMA-to-controller case, real deployments involve multiple AI agents operating concurrently across domains, each with different capabilities and authorities.
identifies multi-agent consensus and long-lived cross-domain orchestration as gaps not addressed by NETCONF.
The trust and authorization aspects of A2A interactions should be coordinated with identity work in the security area, including any Workload Identity Tokens (WIT) work, to avoid redundancy.

section.title: Encoding for AI Agent Consumption
section.t: YANG data returned from NETCONF/RESTCONF operations can be verbose in XML or JSON form, imposing significant token costs when consumed by LLM-based agents. As AI agents become consumers of network management data, encoding efficiency is an operational concern alongside human readability.

A BCP or informational document surveying encoding alternatives -- including CBOR (RFC 8949) and selective field projection -- with guidance on their trade-offs for AI agent use cases, would complement the YANG data model work. This work is suitable for OPSAWG.

section.title: Operational Security BCP
section.t: The security considerations specific to AI-mediated network management are qualitatively different from those of traditional management plane access. They include:

ul.li: The AI service as an attack surface: prompt injection via device-generated telemetry strings included in AI prompts, and compromise of the AI service API endpoint.
Credential management: AI agents require management plane credentials that must not appear in plaintext configuration or AI prompts.
Audit trail integrity: write-ahead logging with integrity protection and forwarding to external aggregators to prevent tampering.
Exfiltration risk: AI agents that send telemetry to external AI services may inadvertently exfiltrate topology, credential, or customer data.

section.t: A BCP document addressing these considerations would give operators deploying AI agents a normative security baseline independent of vendor implementation. This work is suitable for OPSAWG. This work should be coordinated with the DMSC (Dynamic Multi-agent Secured Collaboration) BoF, approved for IETF 126, which addresses security for multi-agent collaboration environments. Emerging OAuth and workload identity work in the security area is relevant to credential management for AI agents and should be referenced.

section.title: Governance Principles as Informational RFC
section.t: The thirteen governance principles defined in are well-developed and provide a sound foundation for implementors. Publishing this document as an Informational RFC would give it stable citable status without requiring it to carry normative weight. The normative content -- the specific configurations and behaviors that implement these principles -- is better expressed in the YANG data models described in .

section.title: AI Agent Lifecycle Management
section.t: An AI agent operating in a network environment has a lifecycle beyond its initial deployment: it must be discoverable by operators and peer agents, its behavior must be verifiable against benchmarks, it may be upgraded as the underlying model or toolset changes, and it may be decommissioned or replaced. In multi-agent systems, these lifecycle events must be coordinated to avoid capability gaps or conflicting authorities.

These concerns map across several venues: discovery belongs with the Discovery of Agents BoF; benchmarking and evaluation methodology belongs in IRTF NMRG, with coordination with the BMWG which has existing work on AI benchmarking; upgrade and operational lifecycle management is suitable for OPSAWG/AIOPS. This document does not propose specific work items for each at this stage but flags them as areas requiring attention in charter development.

section.title: Out of Scope
section.t: The following are explicitly out of scope for the proposed IETF work:

dl.dd: IETF should not produce standalone AI network architecture survey documents. Where a well-developed architecture exists -- such as the NMA architecture in -- IETF work should produce data models and protocol extensions that implement it, not relitigate the architecture. Surveys and taxonomies without an associated deliverable are out of scope.

Specifying which algorithms to use for anomaly detection, classification, or remediation recommendation is outside IETF scope. The YANG data models and protocol extensions proposed here are intentionally AI-service-agnostic.
dl.dt: Gap analyses and surveys without committed deliverables
dl.dd: Gap analyses that are directly coupled to a committed standards-track or BCP work item within the same charter are in scope. Gap analyses that stand alone -- without an associated data model or protocol document -- should not be chartered work items, as the use cases and requirements document is sufficient to scope the normative work.
Guidelines for aligning IETF YANG data models with TMF, 3GPP, or other external frameworks are outside IETF scope and should be addressed through liaison statements rather than chartered work items.

section.title: Summary of Proposed Deliverables
section.t: The following table summarizes the proposed deliverables, organized by IETF-OPS-AD/AINETOPS categories.

tr.td: AI agent audit log information model (YANG)
AI agent operational state information model (YANG)
Subject to community consensus on need
Encoding guidance for AI agent consumption of YANG data
Discovery of Agents BoF (if WG forms); else OPSAWG/AIOPS
CATALIST or Agent Comms BoF (if WG forms); else NMOP
CATALIST or Agent Comms BoF (if WG forms); else NMOP
Discovery of Agents BoF (if WG forms); else OPSAWG/AIOPS
AI agent lifecycle: benchmarking and evaluation
AI agent lifecycle: upgrade and operational management

section.t: Items in the Discovery & Naming and Transport categories are contingent on BoF outcomes. OPSAWG/AIOPS is the fallback home in all cases where a BoF does not produce a working group. Items marked for ONSEN are contingent on ONSEN's charter accommodating them.

section.title: Operational Considerations
section.t: Operators deploying AI agents for network management should ensure that the management interfaces used by AI agents are subject to the same access control, logging, and change management policies as human operator access. The YANG data models proposed in are designed to integrate with existing NETCONF/RESTCONF management infrastructure rather than requiring separate management planes for AI agents.

Deployments using the independent NMA mode -- where an NMA writes directly to device configuration alongside other management systems -- introduce a multiple-writer problem. Operators should enforce single-writer semantics at the orchestration layer, either by routing all configuration changes through the NMA or by using NETCONF private candidates to isolate concurrent sessions. Work items proposing independent NMA deployments should address write-conflict prevention explicitly in their requirements.

section.title: Security Considerations
section.t: The security considerations for AI-mediated network management are discussed in . Key concerns include prompt injection via device-generated data, credential exposure to AI services, audit trail integrity, and telemetry exfiltration. These are addressed in detail in .

section.title: IANA Considerations
section.t: This document has no IANA actions.

front.title: Research Challenges in Coupling Artificial Intelligence and Network Management

section.title: Acknowledgments
section.t: The recommendations in this document were informed by discussions on the OPSAWG and AINETOPS mailing lists, and by the presentations at IETF 125. The governance framework proposed in by Andy Smith and Nalin Pai provided the concrete foundation from which the YANG data model proposals are derived.
